title: Wet-felted hot water bottle cover
subtitle: A felted wool sleeve that insulates a standard hot water bottle and prevents skin contact
excerpt: A wet-felted cover for a standard 2-litre hot water bottle is made by felting two flat panels around the bottle itself as a resist, leaving the neck open. The finished cover acts as insulation, keeping heat in longer, and protects against direct skin contact with hot rubber. Merino felts to a firm but soft fabric appropriate for handling at full bottle temperature.
sourceNotes: Hot water bottle cover felting is a contemporary established technique. The underlying resist-felting method follows standard wet-felting practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: The boundary where two felted panels meet and bond during wet felting. A seam line forms when roving from one panel overlaps roving from the adjacent panel and the two layers felt together.

Wool roving that has been lightly felted to the point where the fibres hold together as a sheet but are not yet fully bonded. Prefelt can be cut to shape and used as inlay decoration or as a flat panel in construction.

content.text: The hot water bottle itself acts as the resist. Roving is laid over the front and back of the bottle in overlapping layers. Where the front and back layers meet at the sides, they felt together to form the
of the cover. The neck of the bottle is left uncovered so the cover can be removed for refilling.

attrs.title: Water temperature
attrs.body: Work with 50-60°C water. Test on the back of the wrist before submerging hands; allow to cool if it stings.

items.name: Merino roving (21-23 micron) in main colour
Merino roving in accent colour (for surface decoration)
Empty 2-litre hot water bottle (clean and dry)

content.text: Step 1: Lay roving on the front face

Lay the bottle flat. Build three crossed layers of roving over the front face of the bottle, extending the edges of each layer 2 to 3 cm beyond the bottle's sides. These overhanging fibres will wrap around and join with the back layers. Leave the neck and stopper ring uncovered.
Step 2: Wet and rub the front face

Cover with the felting net. Drizzle hot soapy water over the roving and rub in circular motions for 5 to 7 minutes. The front face should start to mat and the fibres should cling together when the net is lifted.
Step 3: Turn and lay the back face

Carefully turn the bottle over. The front-face roving should stay attached to it. Fold the overhanging edge fibres from the front face down over the bottle sides. Lay three crossed layers of roving on the back face, extending them over the bottle sides to overlap with the folded front-face fibres. Wet, net, and rub for another 5 to 7 minutes.

Remove the net and rub the cover all over for 15 to 20 minutes, alternating between hot and cold water to shock the fibres. Roll the covered bottle on the bamboo mat for 10 minutes in each direction. The cover will shrink snugly around the bottle.

When the cover is firm and dense, carefully slide the bottle out from the neck opening. This is easiest when the felt is still damp and slightly elastic. Reshape the cover by hand and dry flat. Optionally, needle
decoration onto the dry surface using a needle and foam pad.

Hand-wash the cover in cool water with a small amount of wool wash. Do not machine-wash: the agitation will continue to full the felt, shrinking it further. Dry flat away from direct heat. The cover should outlast several hot water bottles.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Two problems specific to hot water bottle covers.

items.symptom: Cover too tight to slide the bottle in
items.cause: Felt was fulled past the bottle's dimensions before removal.
items.fix: For next time: allow a 15 to 20 per cent shrinkage allowance when laying the initial roving. Once dry, a too-tight cover can be stretched slightly by inserting a slightly smaller cushion form while damp.

items.symptom: Front and back panels separated at the sides
items.cause: The edge fibres from the front face did not overlap adequately with the back-face roving.
items.fix: Ensure that the front-face edge fibres extend at least 2 to 3 cm beyond the bottle sides, and that the back-face layers cover over them completely.
